title: DelegationType enum seed
description: Canonical delegation-type taxonomy. Consumed at boot by `DelegationType` enum registration + by the `valid_delegation_window` observer path. Adding a new type requires a schema migration (delegation_type column is an enum in Postgres) + a coordinated update to the emergency-flow entitlement checker.
columns_note: Fields on each row: `key` (enum member name, snake_case, stable identifier), `display_name` (human-readable label; i18n keys handled separately), `slug` (URL-safe form for API filters), `sort_order` (picker sort ascending), `default_min_notice_hours` (baseline notice period for this type; can be overridden per-tenant), `bypasses_min_notice` (whether this type ignores the tenant's min_advance_notice_hours setting), `requires_entitlement` (which entitlement gates this type).

delegation_types.description: The everyday OOF pattern — user is on vacation / sick / attending a conference and delegates their responsibilities for a bounded window. Symmetric — no expectation of a permanent hand-over. Typical duration: 1-14 days.

Longer-lived transition, e.g. a team lead departing and handing over management responsibilities to their successor while HR completes the formal role reassignment. Typical duration: 14-90 days. The rest of the audit trail treats this differently — SOC 2 evidence review flags handover-type delegations for extra review because they often correlate with employment transitions.

Immediate delegation bypassing the tenant's min_advance_notice_hours setting. Reserved for genuine emergencies (an unplanned illness taking a delegator out mid-day; a security incident requiring immediate access hand-off). Requires the tenant to hold the emergency_delegation entitlement (medium + enterprise). Audited with higher scrutiny — the SOC 2 report highlights emergency delegations by count + rate + duration.

notes: Adding a new type requires: (1) schema migration to extend the Postgres enum on delegation_type column, (2) update this file + regenerate the enum class, (3) update the DelegationType cast, (4) coordinate with the compliance module if the new type has different audit-trail semantics.

The three types share the SAME storage shape — the difference is behavioural: which entitlement gates it + how the observer treats it + how the SOC 2 report labels it. This is deliberate; a new type is a semantic distinction on the same row, not a new table.

The `emergency` type does NOT create audit rows on a separate stream; it stamps the standard delegation row with delegation_type='emergency' + the audit row inherits that label. Compliance reports filter by that label.
